sources.notes: Statement and constraints: interactive DAG path reconstruction with at most 2n queries.
Official editorial confirms querying every vertex's full-set longest path length, then reconstructing by adjacent length layers.

annotation.summary: Given an interactive oracle on a hidden DAG, output any longest path within a linear query budget.
annotation.constraints: sum n <= 1000; each test permits at most 2n oracle queries.
annotation.core_idea: Query each vertex against the full vertex set to get d[v], the longest path length starting there. A maximum d vertex can start a longest path. If the current path endpoint has value k, the next vertex on a longest path must have d value k-1. Test candidates in that layer with a two-vertex query involving the current vertex, and append the one that returns path length 2. Every vertex is tested at most once in reconstruction.
annotation.complexity: O(n) oracle queries and O(n^2) local candidate grouping/filtering
tricks: Use longest-path length as a DAG layer coordinate.
Only search the next path vertex in the d-1 layer.
Probe adjacency/reachability through tiny oracle sets.

solution_variants.name: longest-distance-layer-reconstruction
solution_variants.summary: Query all d[v], start at a maximum-d vertex, then repeatedly test candidates in the next lower d-layer to build the path.

tags.evidence: The main solution reconstructs a hidden path entirely from oracle answers about longest path lengths.
The strict decrease of d[v] along a longest path and the layer reconstruction both rely on DAG acyclicity.
The value d[v] acts as a layer signature used to filter possible successors on the path.
